# Review: `deepmind/405.lean` — Erdős Problem 405

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line (`import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`), immediately after the copyright block, separated by one blank line.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 405`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `https://www.erdosproblems.com/405`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (the Diophantine equation `(p-1)! + a^{p-1} = p^k` and the finiteness question).
- [x] Bibliography entries present for `[ErGr80]`, `[BrEr91]`, and `[YuLi96]`.

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos405 ... end Erdos405`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (`Erdos405`).
- [x] No `open`/`open scoped` statements needed; none present. N/A.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- N/A — no `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_405` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
- [x] Name follows Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` conventions.
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research solved, AMS 11]`.
- [x] Main theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`) referencing `[ErGr80, p.80]`.

### `answer()` usage

- [ ] **ISSUE — Missing `answer()` wrapper (Case 1 violation).**

  The original problem (module docstring, line 24) reads: *"Is it true that the equation $(p-1)! + a^{p-1} = p^k$ has only finitely many solutions?"* — this is a **yes/no question** ("Is it true that …"), which falls under **Case 1**.

  Per the checklist, since the problem has been solved affirmatively (Brindza–Erdős [BrEr91]), the theorem should use:

  ```lean
  theorem erdos_405 : answer(True) ↔
      Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ | ...} := by sorry
  ```

  Currently it is formalized as a **direct assertion** (Case 3 style):

  ```lean
  theorem erdos_405 :
      Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ | ...} := by sorry
  ```

  The `answer(True) ↔ …` wrapper is required by the style guidelines for yes/no problems.

- [x] Quantifiers are not incorrectly placed as theorem parameters (the variables are bound inside the set comprehension via `let`).

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute present.
- [x] Value is `research solved` — valid. The docstring documents that Brindza–Erdős proved finiteness and Yu–Liu gave the complete solution list, so `research solved` is appropriate.
- [x] Not marked `research open` with a `sorry`-free proof. N/A (proof still uses `sorry`, and category is `research solved`).

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute present: `AMS 11`.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (`11` — Number theory).
- [x] Numbers in ascending order. (Only one number.)
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] `11` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses `by sorry` (multi-line form: `:= by\n  sorry`), which is acceptable per the checklist.
- [x] No `sorry` in any `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` (none exist).
- [x] No informal proof sketches for open problems (this is `research solved`).
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- N/A — no variants in this file.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `ℕ`, `×`, `∧`, `≠`, `^`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] Not verified (`lake build` not run as part of this review).

---

## Summary

| Section | Status |
|---|---|
| 1. Copyright Header | PASS |
| 2. Import Statement | PASS |
| 3. Module Docstring | PASS |
| 4. Namespace | PASS |
| 5. Custom Definitions | N/A |
| 6. Theorem Statements | **FAIL** — missing `answer(True) ↔ …` wrapper |
| 7. Category Attribute | PASS |
| 8. AMS Attribute | PASS |
| 9. Proof Bodies | PASS |
| 10. Variants | N/A |
| 11. Code Quality | PASS |
| 12. Build Verification | NOT VERIFIED |

**Verdict: 1 issue to fix before merge.**

The original problem is phrased as a yes/no question ("Is it true that…"), so per the `answer()` usage rules (Case 1), the theorem should be:

```lean
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_405 : answer(True) ↔
    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
      let p := t.1
      let a := t.2.1
      let k := t.2.2
      Nat.Prime p ∧ p ≠ 2 ∧ (p - 1).factorial + a ^ (p - 1) = p ^ k} := by
  sorry
```

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 405

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/405](https://www.erdosproblems.com/405)

Let $p$ be an odd prime. Is it true that the equation
$(p-1)! + a^{p-1} = p^k$
has only finitely many solutions?

Erdős and Graham remark that it is probably true that in general
$(p-1)! + a^{p-1}$ is rarely a power at all (although this can happen,
for example $6! + 2^6 = 28^2$).

Brindza and Erdős [BrEr91] proved that there are finitely many solutions.
Yu and Liu [YuLi96] showed that the only solutions are
$2! + 1^2 = 3$, $2! + 5^2 = 3^3$, and $4! + 1^4 = 5^2$.

[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).

[BrEr91] Brindza, B. and Erdős, P., *On some Diophantine problems involving powers and
factorials*. J. Austral. Math. Soc. Ser. A 51 (1991), 1-7.

[YuLi96] Yu, K. and Liu, L., *On some Diophantine equations involving powers and factorials*.
(1996).
-/

namespace Erdos405

/--
Erdős Problem 405 [ErGr80, p.80]:

There are only finitely many triples $(p, a, k)$ of natural numbers with $p$ an
odd prime such that $(p-1)! + a^{p-1} = p^k$.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_405 :
    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
      let p := t.1
      let a := t.2.1
      let k := t.2.2
      Nat.Prime p ∧ p ≠ 2 ∧ (p - 1).factorial + a ^ (p - 1) = p ^ k} := by
  sorry

end Erdos405

content.text: The fix is straightforward — wrap the statement with `answer(True) ↔`.

  sorry
input.new_string:
theorem erdos_405 : answer(True) ↔
    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
      let p := t.1
      let a := t.2.1
      let k := t.2.2
      Nat.Prime p ∧ p ≠ 2 ∧ (p - 1).factorial + a ^ (p - 1) = p ^ k} := by
  sorry

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 405

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/405](https://www.erdosproblems.com/405)

Let $p$ be an odd prime. Is it true that the equation
$(p-1)! + a^{p-1} = p^k$
has only finitely many solutions?

Erdős and Graham remark that it is probably true that in general
$(p-1)! + a^{p-1}$ is rarely a power at all (although this can happen,
for example $6! + 2^6 = 28^2$).

Brindza and Erdős [BrEr91] proved that there are finitely many solutions.
Yu and Liu [YuLi96] showed that the only solutions are
$2! + 1^2 = 3$, $2! + 5^2 = 3^3$, and $4! + 1^4 = 5^2$.

[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).

[BrEr91] Brindza, B. and Erdős, P., *On some Diophantine problems involving powers and
factorials*. J. Austral. Math. Soc. Ser. A 51 (1991), 1-7.

[YuLi96] Yu, K. and Liu, L., *On some Diophantine equations involving powers and factorials*.
(1996).
-/

namespace Erdos405

/--
Erdős Problem 405 [ErGr80, p.80]:

There are only finitely many triples $(p, a, k)$ of natural numbers with $p$ an
odd prime such that $(p-1)! + a^{p-1} = p^k$.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_405 :
    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
      let p := t.1
      let a := t.2.1
      let k := t.2.2
      Nat.Prime p ∧ p ≠ 2 ∧ (p - 1).factorial + a ^ (p - 1) = p ^ k} := by
  sorry

end Erdos405

content.text: Done. Added the `answer(True) ↔` wrapper to `erdos_405` as required — the problem is a yes/no question ("Is it true that…") that was solved affirmatively, so it falls under Case 1 of the `answer()` usage rules.
